one_line_takeaway: A binary-native, distance-aware white-box evasion attack (sigma-binary + Prioritized Binary Rounding) shows that even state-of-the-art adversarially-trained Android malware defenses like PAD-SMA are far less robust than previously reported (94.6% attack success unbounded, 36.3% at only 10 feature flips).
key_finding: sigma-binary achieves the highest ASR across every defense and every perturbation budget in the study, and it exposes that adversarial-detector defenses previously reported as robust collapse under a properly binary-native attack. On the baseline DNN it reaches ASR10=94.5% and 100% for k>=50. Detector-based defenses (KDE, DLA, DNN+, ICNN) exceed 90% ASR with fewer than 10 modified features and hit 100% by 20 features under adaptive attack. Even PAD-SMA (the strongest defense, previously credited with ~83% accuracy under attack) is driven to 36.3% ASR at k=10 and 94.6% ASR with no budget constraint. Continuous-space attacks (CW, sigma-zero) degrade sharply after binary rounding, confirming that binary-native optimization is what matters. Ablation shows the differentiable Hamming-distance term is the single most important component, while the discriminative feature-importance tie-breaker in PBR is negligible after convergence.
core_contribution: The paper introduces two techniques for adversarial evaluation of ML-based Android malware detectors that operate over binary (present/absent) feature vectors. (1) Prioritized Binary Rounding (PBR): a rounding scheme that converts continuous adversarial perturbations into valid binary features by modifying features in descending order of gradient-derived perturbation magnitude (with discriminative feature importance as a tie-breaker), evaluating after each flip and terminating early once evasion is achieved, thereby preserving attack success while minimizing the number of flipped bits. (2) The sigma-binary attack: a white-box, gradient-based evasion attack tailored to binary domains that optimizes a smooth surrogate combining the detector loss with a differentiable Hamming-distance approximation (sum of delta_i^2/(delta_i^2 + sigma)), plus adaptive sparsity projection, dynamic confidence adjustment, and an outer binary search over a penalty factor C to also evade an auxiliary adversary detector g. Together these produce sparse, binary-valid adversarial examples. A third contribution is a unified, reproducible benchmark comparing the two techniques against many attacks and defenses. NOTE: PBR and sigma-binary are the paper's OWN methods; PGD, CW, BCA/BGA, SMA, Mimicry, sigma-zero, and defenses like PAD-SMA/ICNN/DLA/KDE are prior work re-implemented as baselines, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: White-box, feature-space evasion attack against static Android malware detectors. The attacker has full knowledge of both the malware detector f and any auxiliary adversary detector g (architectures, trained parameters, features), representing a worst-case robustness evaluation. The attacker manipulates the binary feature vector (subject to Hamming-distance/L0 budget k and box constraints), while an approximate inverse-mapping function phi^-1 is assumed to translate feature edits back to functional app-level changes (this mapping is inherited from prior work and NOT re-implemented here). Two sub-settings are distinguished: oblivious attacks (attacker unaware of g, targets only f) and adaptive attacks (attacker explicitly evades both f and g via Lagrangian relaxation). Poisoning and problem-space attacks are explicitly out of scope.
limitations: The paper's own Threats-to-Validity section concedes: (1) the entire evaluation is confined to the binary feature space and relies on the inherited approximate inverse-mapping phi^-1; it does NOT re-implement or verify executable-level functional equivalence of the adversarial examples, so real-world realizability is assumed, not demonstrated. (2) Only one dataset (MalScan, apps from 2011-2018) is used; Drebin was excluded as too small/old, and the authors acknowledge newer corpora (AndroZoo, enterprise data) are needed for generalizability, so results may not transfer to modern malware. (3) The white-box full-knowledge assumption is a worst-case that overstates attacker capability relative to realistic black-box adversaries. (4) Hardware is modest (4GB laptop GPU), and PAD-SMA's ~75x training cost limits the scale at which such defenses can be studied. (5) The improvement over prior attacks is partly a re-evaluation artifact: much of the 'novel vulnerability' finding stems from stronger optimization/more iterations against baselines that prior work under-optimized.

datasets_used: MalScan Android dataset: 30,715 APKs total (15,430 malware, 15,285 benign), spanning 2011-2018
Drebin-style binary feature extraction (Androguard), 8 feature categories, top 10,000 most-frequent features retained; easily-modifiable features excluded
60% train / 20% validation / 20% test split; all 3,104 malicious test samples used for adversarial generation
Drebin malware set (5,560 samples, 2010-2012) considered but excluded as too small/outdated
quantitative_results: sigma-binary vs DNN: ASR10=94.5%, ASR20=99.8%, ASR50/100/inf=100% (best in every column)
sigma-binary vs AT-rFGSMk: ASR10=48.2%, ASR50=90.3%, ASR100=98.8%, ASR_inf=99.4%
sigma-binary vs AT-MaxMA: ASR10=21.9%, ASR100=85.7%, ASR_inf=96.6%
sigma-binary vs PAD-SMA (Table II): ASR10=29.2%, ASR50=84.5%, ASR100=92.2%, ASR_inf=93.9%
Adaptive-attack robustness (Table VII), PAD-SMA: ASR10=36.3%, ASR50=89.8%, ASR_inf=94.6%, median Hamming=13.0
Adaptive-attack, AT-MaxMA: ASR10=23.3% (best low-budget), ASR50=65.2%, ASR_inf=96.6%; AT-rFGSMk ASR10=51.4%
Adaptive-attack, weak detector defenses all near-total: DNN ASR10=94.8%, DNN+=97.5%, ICNN=94.8%, KDE=93.1%, DLA=91.2%, all reaching 100% by k=50; median Hamming ~4.0
Oblivious attack (Table VI), conservative mode: PAD-SMA ASR_inf=3.3% (best) vs ICNN 97.9%, DLA 94.2%, KDE 91.8%, DNN+ 61.4%; deferred mode all 100%
Clean (no-attack) accuracy: DNN 97.4%, DLA 97.2%, KDE/DNN+/AT-rFGSMk 96.2%, AT-MaxMA 94.9%, PAD-SMA 94.4%, ICNN 93.7% (FPR up to 10.6% for ICNN)
Training cost relative to DNN (37 min = 1.0x): AT-rFGSMk 2.8x, AT-MaxMA 8.9x, PAD-SMA ~74.8x (extreme cost for best high-budget robustness)
Efficiency: sigma-binary ~0.45 s/sample and 1.3 GB VRAM against PAD-SMA

Ablation: removing the differentiable Hamming approximation causes the largest low-budget ASR drop (e.g. AT-MaxMA ASR10 23.8%->1.8%) and inflates median perturbation (31->215); PBR feature-importance tie-breaker adds only ~0.1-0.2pp and vanishes after 1,000 iterations
baselines_compared: Attack baselines (prior work): BCA, BGA, Grosse gradient-directed addition, rFGSM, PGD-L1/L2/Linf, iMaxMA, SMA (Stepwise Mixture of Attacks), Mimicry (extended to all benign samples), sigma-zero, Carlini-Wagner (CW L2)

Defense models evaluated (prior work): DNN (baseline), DNN+, KDE, DLA (Dense Layer Analysis), AT-rFGSMk, AT-MaxMA, ICNN, PAD-SMA (Principled Adversarial Malware Detection with Stepwise Mixture of Attacks) -- each tested in conservative and deferred modes where applicable
